name: Bragi
description: Bragi is the Norse god of poetry, music, and eloquence. He is the son of Odin and the giantess Gunnlöð, making him a member of the Æsir. Bragi is renowned for his wisdom, his silver tongue, and his mastery of skaldic verse. He is often depicted as an old man with a long, flowing beard, holding a harp or lyre. Bragi's primary role is as the court poet of Valhalla, where he composes verses and entertains the fallen warriors with tales of their heroic deeds. He is considered the patron god of poets and musicians, inspiring them with his divine talent. Bragi is also the husband of Iðunn, the goddess of youth and keeper of the apples of immortality. His abilities extend beyond mere composition; he is said to possess the power to imbue his words with magical properties, influencing events and shaping the destinies of mortals. Bragi's presence signifies creativity, inspiration, and the enduring power of art. Although not a warrior god, his influence is significant, as he shapes the perception and memory of the gods and heroes through his immortal verse.
subtitle: God of Poetry, Music, and Eloquence

keyMyths.title: Bragi's Origins
keyMyths.description: Bragi is born from the union of Odin and Gunnlöð. Odin seduces Gunnlöð to obtain the mead of poetry, which he then shares with the gods, leading to Bragi's birth and his inherent poetic abilities.

keyMyths.title: Bragi and Loki's Flyting
keyMyths.description: In Lokasenna, Loki engages in a verbal duel with the gods. Bragi attempts to defend the honor of the gods, but Loki mocks him for his perceived lack of courage and skill in battle.

keyMyths.title: Bragi in Valhalla
keyMyths.description: Bragi welcomes fallen warriors to Valhalla with his poetry and music. He helps them adjust to their new lives in the hall of Odin, ensuring their stories are remembered and celebrated.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A major source for Norse mythology, including information on Bragi's role and attributes.
sources.text: A major source for Norse mythology, including information on Bragi's role and attributes.

sources.author: Unknown
sources.description: A collection of Old Norse poems which contains references to Bragi and his interactions with other gods.
sources.text: A collection of Old Norse poems which contains references to Bragi and his interactions with other gods.

worshipPractices.name: Skaldic Performance
worshipPractices.description: While direct worship of Bragi is not widely documented, skalds (poets) likely honored him through their performances. Reciting or composing verses in his name, especially during feasts or gatherings, would serve as a form of tribute.

worshipPractices.name: Libations
worshipPractices.description: Offering libations of mead or ale during poetic recitations or musical performances may have been a way to invoke Bragi's favor and inspiration. The act of sharing drink was central to Norse culture and intertwined with ritual practices.

festivals.name: Winter Nights (Vetrnætr)
festivals.description: Though not explicitly dedicated to Bragi, this festival marked the beginning of winter and was a time for feasting and storytelling. Skalds may have performed in Bragi's honor, seeking his blessing for the long winter months dedicated to indoor pursuits like weaving tales.

festivals.name: Yule
festivals.description: Another major Norse festival, Yule, was a time for great feasts, drinking, and storytelling. Skalds would have been in high demand during Yule, providing entertainment and upholding traditions through verse, indirectly honoring Bragi.

cultural.modernLegacy: Bragi's legacy persists in modern art, literature, and music that draw inspiration from Norse mythology. His name and attributes are sometimes invoked in artistic endeavors and in the study of ancient Scandinavian culture. He represents the power of art and language.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Bragi's parentage marks him as both divine and connected to the primordial forces of the cosmos. He is the son of Odin, the Allfather and chief of the Æsir gods, and Gunnlöð, a giantess who guarded the mead of poetry. Odin seduced Gunnlöð and stole the mead, bringing it back to Asgard. Bragi, therefore, is inherently linked to the source of poetic inspiration itself. While details of his birth are scarce, his origin story highlights the inherent tension and exchange between the gods and the giants, representing the acquisition of knowledge and creative power through cunning and force. This connection to both realms likely grants him a unique perspective and understanding of the world, reflected in his wisdom and eloquence.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Unlike some Norse gods, Bragi does not feature prominently in many surviving myths. His role is more consistent as the court poet in Valhalla, where he welcomes fallen warriors and entertains them with his verses. One notable mention is in the poem *Lokasenna*, where Loki accuses Bragi of cowardice and lack of skill. Bragi, in turn, defends himself with dignity and eloquence, showcasing his wisdom and verbal prowess. This scene underscores his importance in maintaining order and decorum, even in the face of Loki's disruptive nature. While not a warrior or adventurer, Bragi's role is crucial in preserving knowledge, celebrating heroism, and shaping the cultural memory of the Norse people.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Direct evidence of a widespread cult dedicated specifically to Bragi is limited. However, given his role as the god of poetry and eloquence, it is likely that skalds (poets) and those involved in artistic pursuits would have honored him through their work. The act of composing and performing skaldic verse itself could be considered a form of worship, invoking Bragi's inspiration and guidance. Additionally, rituals involving mead or ale, often associated with poetic inspiration, might have been performed in his name. While formal temples or dedicated priests may not have existed, Bragi's influence permeated the cultural practices of the Norse people, particularly those involving language, storytelling, and artistic expression. His presence was felt in every recitation, every song, and every carefully crafted verse.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Bragi's legacy extends beyond the Viking Age, influencing artistic and literary representations of Norse mythology in modern times. He symbolizes the power of language and the importance of preserving cultural heritage through storytelling and verse. His image as an old man with a flowing beard and a harp continues to inspire artists and writers who seek to capture the essence of Norse wisdom and creativity. Furthermore, the study of skaldic poetry provides valuable insights into the beliefs, values, and historical events of the Viking Age, ensuring that Bragi's influence continues to be felt in the realms of academia and popular culture. He remains a potent symbol of the enduring power of art and its capacity to shape our understanding of the world.

shortDescription: Bragi is the Norse god of poetry, music, and eloquence. As the son of Odin, he serves as the skald of Valhalla, master of verse and song.
